beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Nana Coree was a woman whose voice spans the 1940s through the early 1960s. Her sayings are jokes with philosophical punchlines, carrying wisdom inside humor. The Patois elements remain in the lullaby output simplified, but present rather than erased. The dialogue structure of the Caribbean musical tradition is made visible as lyric on the page.

artifactLines: Nana Coree was a woman whose voice spans the 1940s through the early 1960s.
Her sayings are jokes with philosophical punchlines, carrying wisdom inside humor.
The Patois elements remain in the lullaby output simplified, but present rather than erased.
The dialogue structure of the Caribbean musical tradition is made visible as lyric on the page.

beats.narration_text: The idea of preserving Nana Coree's 1940s-to-1960s voice by keeping simplified but present Patois in the lullaby output, treating the Caribbean call-and-response dialogue as visible lyric structure, moves me. I want to understand the craft choices. Can you (1) show how a joke with a philosophical punchline maps into a lullaby line, (2) demonstrate where Patois is simplified yet kept audible without erasing it, and (3) lay out the dialogue structure of the Caribbean musical tradition as an actual lyric template I could fill in?
